thesis: Broadcom is the dominant merchant supplier of custom AI ASICs and networking silicon to hyperscalers, with a rapidly growing optical interconnect business and a stabilizing VMware software segment. Record Q2 FY2026 results and guidance for >$100B AI semiconductor revenue by FY2027 support a durable growth trajectory. The post-earnings pullback improves risk/reward, but near-term expectations remain elevated.

drivers: AI ASIC revenue exceeds $100B by FY2027 with 4+ hyperscaler customers
VMware SaaS transition lifts software margins toward 80%+
800G/1.6T optical interconnect upgrades expand TAM faster than consensus
Networking share gains in AI back-end networks drive switch silicon growth

risks: Hyperscaler AI capex cuts or in-sourcing (Google TPU vertical integration) erode custom silicon TAM
VMware integration churn accelerates, delaying margin recovery
Competition from Marvell (MRVL) and NVIDIA bundles squeezes market share
Macro semiconductor down-cycle compresses multiples to 20–22x

base_case.assumptions: Steady AI ASIC growth at 25–30% CAGR; VMware stabilizes with modest churn; networking/optical grows in line with data center capex; stock trades at 28–32x forward earnings.

catalysts.event: Q3 FY2026 earnings — AI revenue guidance update critical
VMware annual conference — subscription transition metrics
FY2026 year-end results and FY2027 guidance

key_risks: Expectations mismatch: stock dropped >12% post-Q2 despite record results; market may punish any guidance shortfall
Hyperscaler concentration: Google, Meta, Microsoft drive majority of AI semiconductor revenue
VMware integration risk: customer churn and sales disruption from product rationalization
Competition from Marvell in custom AI silicon and from NVIDIA in training workloads
Macro cyclicality: AI capex is not immune to broader semiconductor demand cycles
